text: The lower esophageal sphincter or LES is a one-way valve that separates the esophagus from the stomach. When food or liquid is swallowed, the LES opens transiently to allow passage into the stomach. Otherwise, the sphincter remains closed to prevent stomach contents from flowing back up into the esophagus.

However, for patients who suffer from reflux, the LES abnormally remains open allowing stomach secretions and contents to reflux up into the esophagus causing symptoms. A hiatal hernia may also be present further promoting abnormal reflux.

A hiatal hernia is when the top of the stomach gets displaced or herniates through the diaphragm and up into the chest cavity. Given this anatomic shift, the overall esophageal length is now shorter. Additionally, the lower esophageal sphincter is no longer supported by the diaphragm to help with its sphincteric function. Normally, the LES is positioned at the same level as the diaphragm.

In order to maximally control reflux surgically, both the hiatal hernia and the abnormally relaxed LES must be corrected. First, the hiatal hernia is repaired. Surgical repair essentially involves pulling the stomach back into normal position fully below the diaphragm and making the opening in the diaphragm smaller. A mesh is then placed over the incision to reinforce the closure to minimize risk of hiatal hernia recurrence. The LES is then tightened by wrapping the top part of the stomach completely around the sphincter like a belt. This three hundred sixty degree stomach wrap is known as the Nissen fundoplication.
The surgical footage shows the dissection of the stomach, esophagus, and hernia sac from surrounding chest and abdominal soft tissue structures during hiatal hernia repair.

The surgeon carefully mobilizes the hernia sac and esophagus, ensuring identification and preservation of the vagal nerves. The esophagus is dissected to achieve at least three centimeters of distal esophageal length within the abdomen, using a high mediastinal dissection technique to prevent recurrence.

The surgeon closes the enlarged diaphragmatic opening, or hiatus, with sutures to achieve a tension-free repair. A mesh is then placed over the incision to reinforce the closure, reducing the risk of hiatal hernia recurrence.

The esophagus and stomach are visible, prepared for surgical manipulation. A rubber band encircles the lower esophageal sphincter (LES), with the esophagus positioned above and the stomach below. A white mesh is visible at the top, indicating the site of a previous hiatal hernia repair. The surgeon pushes the top part of the stomach behind and around the esophagus, moving it back and forth to ensure it is not too tight, which could restrict blood flow. The stomach wrap is then sutured, with the first suture connecting stomach tissue to stomach tissue, the second suture passing through the esophagus, and the third suture through the LES.
The surgeon completes the Nissen fundoplication, finalizing the 360-degree wrap of the stomach around the lower esophageal sphincter to enhance reflux control.
